exercicios.pergunta: ___ I known about the traffic, I would have left earlier.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had I known' is an inverted/formal form of 'If I had known' — used in written/literary English. 'Have I known' and 'Did I known' are grammatically incorrect. 'If' alone without the inversion is also correct but the blank demands the inverted auxiliary.

exercicios.pergunta: If they ___ the instructions carefully, the machine wouldn't have broken.
exercicios.explicacao: In the Third Conditional the if-clause needs Past Perfect (had + past participle). 'Followed' gives Second Conditional meaning (unreal present). 'Would have followed' can NEVER go in the if-clause.

exercicios.pergunta: She ___ the job if she had prepared better for the interview.
exercicios.explicacao: The result clause of the Third Conditional requires 'would have + past participle'. 'Would get' is Second Conditional. 'Had got' is Past Perfect, not a result clause. 'Got' is simple past.

exercicios.pergunta: Which sentence is a correct Third Conditional?
opcoes: If I would have known, I had told you.
If I had known, I would have told you.
If I knew, I would have told you.
Had I know, I would have told you.
exercicios.explicacao: Only option B follows the full pattern: if + past perfect / would have + past participle. A puts 'would have' in the if-clause (forbidden). C mixes 2nd and 3rd conditionals. D uses 'know' instead of 'known' (bare infinitive, wrong).

exercicios.pergunta: If we ___ the map, we wouldn't have got lost.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had checked' is the Past Perfect required in the Third Conditional if-clause. 'Checked' (simple past) would signal Second Conditional. 'Have checked' is Present Perfect — wrong tense. 'Check' is present — also wrong.

exercicios.pergunta: He didn't call her. She didn't know he was in town. What is the correct Third Conditional?
opcoes: If he had called her, she would know he was in town.
If he called her, she would have known.
If he had called her, she would have known he was in town.
If he has called her, she would have known.
exercicios.explicacao: Both parts must reflect unreal past: if + past perfect → would have + past participle. A switches the result to present (Second Conditional result). B has simple past in if-clause (Second Conditional). D uses present perfect in if-clause.

exercicios.pergunta: ___ they invested in that company, they would have made a fortune.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had they invested' is the formal inversion of 'If they had invested'. 'Should they invest' is an inversion of First Conditional (real future possibility). 'Have they invested' and 'If' alone without the past perfect do not complete the inversion correctly.

exercicios.pergunta: The accident ___ if the driver had been paying attention.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Wouldn't have happened' is the negative result clause for Third Conditional. 'Wouldn't happen' is Second Conditional. 'Hadn't happened' is Past Perfect — cannot be the result clause. 'Won't have happened' is future perfect — wrong context.

exercicios.pergunta: If I had taken that job offer, I ___ living in New York right now. (mixed conditional)
exercicios.explicacao: This is a Mixed Conditional: a past unreal condition (if I had taken) with a present result (would be — how things stand now). 'Would have been' is pure Third Conditional and implies a past result. 'Had been' and 'am' don't form conditional results.

exercicios.pergunta: Which word CANNOT be used in a Third Conditional if-clause?
exercicios.explicacao: 'Would have' must NEVER appear in the if-clause of any conditional. It belongs only in the result (main) clause. 'Had', 'hadn't', and 'been' can all appear in past perfect constructions inside the if-clause.

exercicios.pergunta: If the weather ___ so bad, we would have gone camping.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Hadn't been' is the Past Perfect needed for the Third Conditional. 'Wasn't' (simple past) would make it Second Conditional. 'Isn't' is present. 'Wouldn't have been' cannot go in the if-clause.

exercicios.pergunta: I would have emailed you ___ I had your address.
exercicios.explicacao: 'If' introduces the conditional clause here in standard word order (would have + if + past perfect). 'Unless' means 'if not' and would reverse the logic. 'Provided' needs a present or modal context. 'Had' would require inversion: 'Had I had your address, I would have emailed you.'

exercicios.pergunta: If she hadn't missed the deadline, she ___ the scholarship.
exercicios.explicacao: Past unreal condition (hadn't missed) requires a past unreal result: would have + past participle. 'Would receive' = Second Conditional (present). 'Had received' is Past Perfect, not a result clause. 'Will receive' is a real future.

exercicios.pergunta: The correct meaning of 'If I had left at 8, I would have caught the train' is:
opcoes: I left at 8 and caught the train.
I didn't leave at 8 and I missed the train.
I might leave at 8 and catch the train.
I will leave at 8 and catch the train.
exercicios.explicacao: Third Conditional describes an unreal (counterfactual) past. The implication is: I did NOT leave at 8 (so I missed the train). It is backward-looking regret/speculation.

exercicios.pergunta: ___known it was going to rain, I would have brought an umbrella.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had I known' is the correct formal inversion. 'If I had known' is also correct but the blank must fit without 'If' preceding it. 'I had known' is not a clause opener. 'If had I' is inverted incorrectly.

exercicios.pergunta: We could have avoided the problem if someone ___ us.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had warned' is the Past Perfect for the if-clause. 'Warned' (simple past) makes it Second Conditional. 'Would warn' and 'would have warned' cannot appear in if-clauses.

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite using inversion (no 'if'): 'If I had realized the risk, I wouldn't have invested.' →
exercicios.resposta: Had I realized the risk, I wouldn't have invested.
exercicios.dica: Had + subject + past participle...
exercicios.explicacao: Remove 'if' and move 'had' before the subject: 'Had I realized...' The rest of the sentence stays the same. This formal inversion is common in written English.

exercicios.pergunta: Complete: 'If they ______ (not / waste) so much time, they would have finished the project.' →
exercicios.resposta: hadn't wasted
exercicios.dica: Negative Past Perfect = had not + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: The negative Past Perfect 'hadn't wasted' is required for the if-clause in the Third Conditional. The contraction 'hadn't' is standard.

exercicios.pergunta: Correct the error: 'If I would have seen her, I would have said hello.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I had seen her, I would have said hello.
exercicios.dica: Never 'would have' in the if-clause.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Would have' must be removed from the if-clause and replaced with the Past Perfect 'had seen'. The rule: 'would' NEVER goes in the if-clause.

exercicios.pergunta: Translate using the Third Conditional: 'Se eu tivesse estudado mais, teria passado no exame.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I had studied more, I would have passed the exam.
exercicios.dica: if + had + p.p. / would have + p.p.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Tivesse estudado' = had studied (Past Perfect). 'Teria passado' = would have passed. The structure mirrors the Portuguese subjunctive past perfectly.

exercicios.pergunta: Make negative: 'If he had apologized, she would have forgiven him.' → (he did NOT apologize) →
exercicios.resposta: If he hadn't apologized, she wouldn't have forgiven him.
exercicios.dica: Negate both clauses: hadn't... / wouldn't have...
exercicios.explicacao: To negate the Third Conditional, add 'not' to both the Past Perfect in the if-clause (hadn't) and the modal in the result clause (wouldn't have).

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite as a question: 'Would you have accepted the offer if you had known the salary?' — Now answer it as a negative Third Conditional sentence:
exercicios.resposta: I wouldn't have accepted the offer if I had known the salary.
exercicios.dica: subject + wouldn't have + p.p. + if + had + p.p.
exercicios.explicacao: Invert the question back to declarative. Negative result: wouldn't have accepted. Condition: if I had known.

exercicios.pergunta: Combine into a Third Conditional: 'She didn't back up her files. She lost all her work.' →
exercicios.resposta: If she had backed up her files, she wouldn't have lost all her work.
exercicios.dica: Cause → if + had + p.p. / Effect negated → wouldn't have + p.p.
exercicios.explicacao: The first sentence becomes the if-clause (had backed up). The second becomes the negated result clause (wouldn't have lost), showing what WOULD have happened.

exercicios.pergunta: Fill both gaps: 'If the surgeon ___ (be) more experienced, the patient ___ (survive).' →
exercicios.resposta: had been / would have survived
exercicios.dica: if-clause: had + p.p. | result: would have + p.p.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had been' is the Past Perfect of 'be'. 'Would have survived' is the result clause. Both gaps follow the standard Third Conditional pattern.

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite using inversion: 'If we hadn't arrived late, we would have got good seats.' →
exercicios.resposta: Had we not arrived late, we would have got good seats.
exercicios.dica: Had + subject + not + past participle...
exercicios.explicacao: In negative inversions, 'not' stays after the subject: 'Had we not arrived' (NOT 'Hadn't we arrived' — that contraction is not used in formal inversion).

exercicios.pergunta: Correct and explain: 'If I had study harder, I would of passed.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I had studied harder, I would have passed.
exercicios.dica: Two errors: 'study' → 'studied'; 'would of' → 'would have'
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had study' is wrong — needs past participle: 'had studied'. 'Would of' is a spelling mistake based on pronunciation — the correct form is always 'would have' (often contracted to 'would've').

exercicios.pergunta: Transform into a question using inversion: 'If you had known, would you have told me?' →
exercicios.resposta: Had you known, would you have told me?
exercicios.dica: Remove 'if' and front 'had'
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had you known' replaces 'If you had known' in the inverted form. The result clause question structure stays: would you have told me?

exercicios.pergunta: Complete: 'If it ___ (not rain) so heavily, the match wouldn't have been cancelled.'
exercicios.resposta: hadn't rained
exercicios.explicacao: Past Perfect negative in the if-clause: 'hadn't rained'. Third Conditional describes the unreal past condition — it DID rain heavily in reality.

exercicios.pergunta: Complete with the correct form: '___she taken the earlier flight, she would have made it to the meeting.'
exercicios.resposta: Had
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had she taken' is the formal inversion of 'If she had taken'. 'Had' fronts the clause, removing 'if'. This is literary/formal style often seen in business writing.

exercicios.pergunta: What is wrong? 'If I have studied more, I would have passed.' →
exercicios.resposta: have studied → had studied
exercicios.explicacao: The if-clause needs Past Perfect ('had studied'), not Present Perfect ('have studied'). Present Perfect cannot express an unreal past condition in English.

exercicios.pergunta: If I had studied medicine, I ___ a doctor now.
exercicios.explicacao: This is a Mixed Conditional: past unreal condition (if I had studied) + present result (would be — how things stand now). 'Would have been' gives a pure Third Conditional past result. 'Had been' is Past Perfect. 'Am' is simple present, not conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: If she were more organized, she ___ all those mistakes last week.
exercicios.explicacao: Second type: present unreal condition (if she were) + past result (wouldn't have made). The condition is about her permanent character (present), the result is about last week (past). This is the reverse mixed conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: Which is a correct Mixed Conditional?
opcoes: If I had been born in the USA, I speak English fluently.
If I had been born in the USA, I would speak English fluently.
If I were born in the USA, I would have spoken English fluently.
If I had been born in the USA, I would have spoken English fluently.
exercicios.explicacao: B: past unreal condition → present unreal result (would speak). A lacks a modal. C mixes wrong tenses — 'were born' + 'would have spoken' is inconsistent. D is pure Third Conditional (both past).

exercicios.pergunta: If he ___ more ambitious, he wouldn't have turned down that promotion.
exercicios.explicacao: The result 'wouldn't have turned down' is past, so the condition refers to a present personality trait using Second Conditional form: 'if he were'. This is Mixed Conditional Type 2 (present condition → past result). 'Had been' would make it pure Third Conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: If I hadn't moved to Brazil, I ___ Portuguese now.
exercicios.explicacao: Past unreal condition (hadn't moved) + present result (wouldn't speak now). The word 'now' confirms a present state result. 'Wouldn't have spoken' = pure Third Conditional (past result). The mixed conditional links past cause to present reality.

exercicios.pergunta: She failed the interview because she is very nervous. Mixed conditional: If she ___ so nervous, she ___ the interview.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Weren't' (present character trait — she IS nervous by nature) + 'wouldn't have failed' (past event — the interview already happened). This is the present-condition-to-past-result mixed conditional. Option D reverses the logic incorrectly.

exercicios.pergunta: If I ___ so tired right now, I would have joined you last night.
exercicios.explicacao: Present condition (I feel tired now → weren't) + past result (last night → would have joined). 'Weren't' is the subjunctive/hypothetical present form. 'Hadn't been' would put both clauses in the past (pure Third Conditional).

exercicios.pergunta: The sentence 'If I had taken better care of my health, I would be feeling great now' is:
exercicios.explicacao: The if-clause is past unreal (had taken — Third Conditional pattern), but the result is present (would be feeling — Second Conditional pattern). Past cause → present result = Mixed Conditional Type 1 (past → present).

exercicios.pergunta: Which word signals that a Mixed Conditional result clause refers to the PRESENT?
exercicios.explicacao: Time words like 'now', 'today', 'at the moment', 'still' in the result clause signal that the consequence is a present state — indicating the Mixed Conditional (past condition → present result).

exercicios.pergunta: He doesn't know how to swim. That's why he nearly drowned last summer. Correct mixed conditional:
opcoes: If he knew how to swim, he wouldn't nearly drown.
If he had known how to swim, he wouldn't have nearly drowned.
If he knew how to swim, he wouldn't have nearly drowned.
If he had known how to swim, he wouldn't nearly drown.
exercicios.explicacao: Present condition (he doesn't know = permanent fact → if he knew) + past result (last summer → wouldn't have nearly drowned). Option B is pure Third Conditional. Option A is Second Conditional (both present). Option D reverses the tenses incorrectly.

exercicios.pergunta: If she hadn't grown up abroad, her English ___ as fluent as it is now.
exercicios.explicacao: The result 'as it is now' anchors this in the present: her current English level is the consequence of having grown up abroad (past event). Result clause = wouldn't be (present). Pure Third Conditional would give 'wouldn't have been'.

exercicios.pergunta: If I ___ allergic to cats, I would have adopted that kitten you found.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Weren't' is the hypothetical present (I AM allergic now — permanent trait). The result is past ('would have adopted' — referring to the kitten-finding event). Present condition → past result = Mixed Conditional. 'Hadn't been' would imply the allergy ended.

exercicios.pergunta: Which two conditionals combine to form a Mixed Conditional?
exercicios.explicacao: A Mixed Conditional combines the if-clause of one type with the result clause of another, specifically mixing Second (unreal present) and Third (unreal past) elements in either order.

exercicios.pergunta: If he had accepted that job in Tokyo, he ___ Japanese much better today.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Would speak' (present ability/state) + 'today' confirms a present result from a past decision. This is the classic past-condition-to-present-result mixed conditional. 'Would have spoken' would be correct for a pure Third Conditional past result.

exercicios.pergunta: ___ more careful by nature, she wouldn't have signed that contract without reading it.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Were she more careful' is the formal inversion of 'If she were more careful' — present hypothetical condition. Result is past (wouldn't have signed), making this a Mixed Conditional. 'Had she been' = past condition (Third Conditional inversion).

exercicios.pergunta: If they ___ the terms and conditions, they wouldn't be in legal trouble now.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had read' = past unreal action. 'Now' in the result clause = present consequence. This is the most common Mixed Conditional pattern: past unreal → present result. 'Read' (simple past) would be Second Conditional. 'Have read' is Present Perfect.

exercicios.pergunta: Combine using a Mixed Conditional: 'He didn't study law. He can't advise you on this legal issue.' →
exercicios.resposta: If he had studied law, he would be able to advise you on this legal issue.
exercicios.dica: Past cause → present consequence
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had studied' (past unreal condition) + 'would be able to' (present result/ability). 'Can't advise you' becomes 'wouldn't be able to advise you' in the negative.

exercicios.pergunta: Identify which type: 'If I were taller, I would have made the basketball team last year.' →
exercicios.resposta: Mixed Conditional (present condition → past result)
exercicios.dica: Check: is the condition present or past? Is the result present or past?
exercicios.explicacao: 'If I were taller' = present hypothetical (I am short — permanent trait). 'Would have made' = past result (last year's tryout). Present condition → past result = Mixed Conditional Type 2.

exercicios.pergunta: Correct: 'If I had more confidence, I would have spoke in public better.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I had more confidence, I would have spoken in public better.
exercicios.dica: Check the past participle of 'speak'
exercicios.explicacao: 'Spoke' is simple past. The past participle is 'spoken'. In 'would have + p.p.', you need 'spoken'. The condition 'if I had more confidence' is present hypothetical, making this a Mixed Conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: Translate: 'Se eu fosse mais extrovertido, teria feito mais amigos na escola.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I were more extroverted, I would have made more friends at school.
exercicios.dica: Present trait (were) → past result (would have made)
exercicios.explicacao: 'Fosse' = subjunctive of 'ser' = were (present hypothetical). 'Teria feito' = would have made (past result). This is a Mixed Conditional: current personality trait → past consequence.

exercicios.pergunta: Fill both gaps: 'If she ___ (live) in Italy all her life, she ___ (know) how to make proper pasta now.' →
exercicios.resposta: had lived / would know
exercicios.dica: Past condition → present result
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had lived' (Past Perfect) = past unreal condition. 'Would know' (present modal) = present result. The word 'now' confirms the result is about the present state.

exercicios.pergunta: Write a Mixed Conditional using: past condition = 'win the lottery last year', present result = 'not work anymore' →
exercicios.resposta: If I had won the lottery last year, I wouldn't be working anymore.
exercicios.dica: if + had + p.p. ... wouldn't + be + -ing (present continuous for ongoing state)
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had won' = Third Conditional if-clause. 'Wouldn't be working' = present continuous showing current ongoing state (Second Conditional result pattern).

exercicios.pergunta: Which sentence shows the REVERSE mixed conditional (present→past)? Write it out: condition = 'be a night owl (present trait)', result = 'fall asleep during the meeting yesterday' →
exercicios.resposta: If she weren't such a night owl, she wouldn't have fallen asleep during the meeting yesterday.
exercicios.dica: Present trait (weren't) → past event (wouldn't have fallen)
exercicios.explicacao: 'Weren't' = present hypothetical (she IS a night owl). 'Wouldn't have fallen asleep' = past result (yesterday's meeting). This reverses the usual order: present character → past consequence.

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite as Mixed Conditional: 'He is not a native speaker, so he misunderstood the joke at the party.' →
exercicios.resposta: If he were a native speaker, he wouldn't have misunderstood the joke at the party.
exercicios.dica: Permanent present trait → specific past event
exercicios.explicacao: Being a non-native speaker is a present characteristic (if he were). Misunderstanding the joke was a past event (wouldn't have misunderstood). Present condition → past result.

exercicios.pergunta: Correct all errors: 'If I would be richer, I had bought that house last year.' →
exercicios.resposta: If I were richer, I would have bought that house last year.
exercicios.dica: Two errors: 'would be' in if-clause; 'had bought' as result
exercicios.explicacao: 'Would be' cannot go in the if-clause — use 'were' (hypothetical present). 'Had bought' looks like Past Perfect, not a conditional result — change to 'would have bought'.

exercicios.pergunta: Explain in one sentence why: 'If I had been born in Spain, I would speak Spanish' is Mixed and not Second Conditional.
exercicios.resposta: Because the if-clause refers to an unreal past event (birth) while the result refers to a present state (speaking ability now).
exercicios.dica: Focus on the time reference of each clause
exercicios.explicacao: Second Conditional uses simple past in the if-clause (if I were). Here 'had been born' is Past Perfect — an unreal past event whose present consequence is current language ability. That combination = Mixed Conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: Complete: 'Were she more patient, she ___ (not / lose) her temper with the client yesterday.' →
exercicios.resposta: wouldn't have lost
exercicios.dica: Formal inversion present condition → past result
exercicios.explicacao: 'Were she more patient' is the inverted form of 'If she were more patient' (present trait). The past event (yesterday) requires 'wouldn't have lost' in the result clause — Mixed Conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: Complete: 'If he ___ (not / drop out) of university, he would have a degree now.'
exercicios.resposta: hadn't dropped out
exercicios.explicacao: 'Hadn't dropped out' is Past Perfect negative. This is a Mixed Conditional: past unreal action (dropping out) → present consequence (having/not having a degree). 'Now' confirms the result is present.

exercicios.pergunta: Is this correct? 'If I had more sleep last night, I would feel better now.' Correct if not.
exercicios.resposta: If I had slept more last night, I would feel better now.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had more sleep' is not a past perfect construction — it needs 'had slept'. The result 'would feel better now' is correct for the Mixed Conditional (past sleep → present feeling). Alternative: 'If I had had more sleep' is also grammatically valid but less natural.

exercicios.pergunta: Write the Mixed Conditional for: 'I am very shy. That's why I didn't speak at the conference.'
exercicios.resposta: If I weren't so shy, I would have spoken at the conference.
exercicios.explicacao: Shyness is a present personality trait ('if I weren't so shy' — Second Conditional if-clause). Not speaking at the conference was a past event ('would have spoken' — Third Conditional result). Present trait → past consequence = Mixed Conditional.

exercicios.pergunta: The new museum ___ by the end of next year.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Will be built' is the Future Simple Passive. 'Will build' is active. 'Is built' is Present Simple Passive (wrong tense). 'Has been built' is Present Perfect Passive — implies completion already.

exercicios.pergunta: A new policy ___ right now to address the issue.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Is being developed' is the Present Continuous Passive (am/is/are + being + past participle) showing an action in progress now. 'Is developing' is active. 'Has been developed' = already complete. 'Was developed' = past.

exercicios.pergunta: The documents ___ by the time you arrive.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Will have been signed' is the Future Perfect Passive — completed before a future reference point ('by the time you arrive'). 'Will be signed' is Future Simple Passive (no 'by the time' implication of completion before). 'Are being signed' = happening now.

exercicios.pergunta: The suspect ___ questioned by detectives for three hours before being released.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Had been questioned' is Past Perfect Passive — the questioning was completed before another past event (being released). 'Was being questioned' = Past Continuous Passive (incomplete, in progress). 'Has been' = Present Perfect.

exercicios.pergunta: All applications ___ submitted online. No paper forms accepted.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Must be submitted' is Modal Passive (modal + be + past participle). 'Must submit' is active. 'Must be submitting' is wrong (passive continuous with modal doesn't work this way for obligation). 'Have been submitted' is Perfect Passive (no modal).

exercicios.pergunta: This report ___ before Monday.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Should be finished' = modal passive for obligation/recommendation. 'Should finish' is active. 'Should have finished' = modal perfect (implies it SHOULD have been done already but wasn't). 'Should be finishing' = progressive (about an ongoing action, not completion).

exercicios.pergunta: ___ that the prime minister will resign next week.
exercicios.explicacao: 'It is said that...' is the impersonal passive construction. 'It says' is active (like a book). 'They are said' would need: 'They are said to be planning...' — but 'They are said that...' is wrong (can't use 'that' after personal passive subject). 'It has said' is active present perfect.

exercicios.pergunta: She ___ the best candidate for the position.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Is considered to be' is the personal impersonal passive with passive infinitive. 'Is considering to be' is wrong (consider + to-infinitive only in active). 'Considers to be' is active. 'Has been considering' = Present Perfect active (she is doing the considering).

exercicios.pergunta: The bridge ___ for months before it finally collapsed.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Was being repaired' = Past Continuous Passive — an ongoing process in the past. 'Had been repaired' = Past Perfect Passive (completed before). 'Had been being repaired' is grammatically possible but extremely rare and awkward. 'Was repairing' = active.

exercicios.pergunta: The error ___ to have occurred during the software update.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Appears to have occurred' uses a passive infinitive in a semi-passive reporting structure. 'Is appeared' is wrong — 'appear' is not used in passive voice in English. 'Appeared to have occurred' (simple past) is also acceptable but the present 'appears' is more natural for current assessment.

exercicios.pergunta: The concert tickets can ___ from the official website.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Can be purchased' = modal (can) + be + past participle. 'Be purchase' omits the -d. 'Purchased' without 'be' can't follow 'can' as a passive. 'Be purchasing' is wrong (progressive passive after modal has different meaning).

exercicios.pergunta: Passive of 'They were interviewing candidates all day': →
opcoes: Candidates were being interviewed all day.
Candidates had been interviewed all day.
Candidates are being interviewed all day.
exercicios.explicacao: Past Continuous Passive: was/were + being + past participle. 'Were interviewed all day' (Past Simple Passive) could imply a completed single event. 'Were being interviewed' emphasizes the ongoing process throughout the day.

exercicios.pergunta: It ___ that thousands of people were affected by the earthquake.
exercicios.explicacao: 'It is estimated that...' = impersonal passive reporting structure. 'Is estimating' = active progressive. 'Estimates' = active (it/he/she estimates — who?). 'Has estimated' = active perfect.

exercicios.pergunta: The CEO is ___ to announce the merger tomorrow.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Is expected to announce' = personal passive with passive infinitive. 'Is expecting to announce' = active (he is planning). 'Is being expected' = continuous passive (not idiomatic here). 'Is be expected' is grammatically impossible.

exercicios.pergunta: All visitors ___ to sign in at reception.
exercicios.explicacao: 'Are required to' = passive modal equivalent (obligation). 'Require' = active (visitors require something else). 'Required' = simple past. 'Have required' = active perfect (visitors have required something).

exercicios.pergunta: Which sentence correctly uses the passive infinitive?
opcoes: She expected to be promoted soon.
She is being expected to promote.
exercicios.explicacao: 'To be promoted' = passive infinitive (someone else does the promoting). 'To promote' = active infinitive (she promotes others). 'Expected promoting' is wrong (expect + to-infinitive, not gerund). 'Is being expected to promote' is active — she is expected to promote others.

exercicios.pergunta: Change to passive (keep the same tense): 'Engineers are currently redesigning the safety system.' →
exercicios.resposta: The safety system is currently being redesigned by engineers.
exercicios.dica: is/are + being + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: Present Continuous Passive: 'is being redesigned'. The agent 'by engineers' is optional but included here. 'Currently' stays in position before or after the auxiliary.

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite using impersonal passive: 'People believe that the economy will recover soon.' →
exercicios.resposta: It is believed that the economy will recover soon.
exercicios.dica: It + passive reporting verb + that-clause
exercicios.explicacao: 'Believe' becomes 'is believed'. The original subject 'people' is dropped. The 'that-clause' stays unchanged. Alternative: 'The economy is believed to recover soon' (personal passive + infinitive).

exercicios.pergunta: Transform: 'Someone must check these calculations.' →
exercicios.resposta: These calculations must be checked.
exercicios.dica: modal + be + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: Modal Passive: 'must be checked'. The vague agent 'someone' is dropped. The object 'these calculations' becomes the subject.

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite as personal impersonal passive: 'It is thought that she is living abroad.' →
exercicios.resposta: She is thought to be living abroad.
exercicios.dica: Subject + is/are + reporting verb (p.p.) + to + infinitive
exercicios.explicacao: 'She is thought to be living' uses the personal passive construction. The present participle 'living' stays because the subordinate clause action is simultaneous with the main verb. Passive infinitive: 'to be living'.

exercicios.pergunta: Correct the passive: 'The report has being written by the team.' →
exercicios.resposta: The report has been written by the team.
exercicios.dica: Present Perfect Passive: has/have + been + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: Present Perfect Passive = has/have + BEEN (not 'being') + past participle. 'Has being' is never correct. 'Being' is only used after 'is/are/was/were' for continuous passives.

exercicios.pergunta: Transform to passive: 'They should have informed us about the changes.' →
exercicios.resposta: We should have been informed about the changes.
exercicios.dica: modal perfect passive: should have been + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: Modal Perfect Passive = should have been + past participle. 'Us' becomes 'we' (subject of passive). The agent 'they' can be omitted.

exercicios.pergunta: Write the Past Continuous Passive: 'Workmen were laying new cables under the street.' →
exercicios.resposta: New cables were being laid under the street.
exercicios.dica: was/were + being + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: Past Continuous Passive: 'were being laid'. 'Lay' → 'laid' (irregular). The agent 'by workmen' is optional. This shows an action in progress at a past time.

exercicios.pergunta: Make passive using impersonal structure: 'Experts claim that rising sea levels threaten coastal cities.' →
exercicios.resposta: It is claimed that rising sea levels threaten coastal cities.
exercicios.dica: It + is/are + past participle + that-clause
exercicios.explicacao: 'Claim' → 'is claimed'. The reporting verb goes passive; the 'that-clause' content stays. This is the impersonal passive used in academic/journalistic writing to distance from the source.

exercicios.pergunta: Form a passive with modal: 'You can improve your score by practising daily.' →
exercicios.resposta: Your score can be improved by practising daily.
exercicios.dica: modal + be + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: 'Can be improved' = Modal Passive. The object 'your score' becomes subject. 'By practising' remains as it describes method, not a human agent (so it stays).

exercicios.pergunta: Passive infinitive: 'The board expects that they will elect a new CEO next month.' Rewrite using passive infinitive →
exercicios.resposta: A new CEO is expected to be elected next month.
exercicios.dica: Subject + is expected + to be + past participle
exercicios.explicacao: 'To be elected' is the passive infinitive. The board's expectation becomes the main passive verb 'is expected'. The future element is expressed in the passive infinitive structure.

exercicios.pergunta: Identify and name the passive: 'The paintings are said to have been stolen decades ago.' →
exercicios.resposta: Impersonal passive with perfect passive infinitive (to have been stolen)
exercicios.dica: Look for: impersonal structure + perfect infinitive passive
exercicios.explicacao: 'Are said' = impersonal passive reporting verb. 'To have been stolen' = perfect passive infinitive (action completed before the present reporting time). This double-passive structure is common in journalism.

exercicios.pergunta: Complete: 'The annual budget ___ (review) by the board every December.'
exercicios.resposta: is reviewed
exercicios.explicacao: Present Simple Passive for regular/habitual actions: is + reviewed. The board reviews it regularly, so present simple passive is correct. Not 'is being reviewed' (which would suggest it's happening right now).

exercicios.pergunta: Rewrite: 'People say she works 16 hours a day.' → 'She ___'
exercicios.resposta: She is said to work 16 hours a day.
exercicios.explicacao: Personal impersonal passive: 'She is said to + base infinitive'. The present infinitive 'to work' is used because the working is simultaneous with the saying. Use perfect infinitive 'to have worked' only if the working happened BEFORE the saying.

exercicios.pergunta: Correct: 'A decision will been announced tomorrow.' →
exercicios.resposta: A decision will be announced tomorrow.
exercicios.explicacao: Future Simple Passive = will + BE (not 'been') + past participle. 'Been' is only used after 'have' (has been, had been). After 'will', always use the base form 'be'.
